items.question: Why use cross-validation for model selection on small datasets?
items.answer: A single validation split is noisy when n is small—the winning model may overfit that partition's quirks. CV averages performance across k folds on train+val, reducing split luck during comparison. After selection, refit on all development data and evaluate once on a locked test set.

items.question: What is wrong with reporting GridSearchCV best_score_ as your final performance?
items.answer: best_score_ is the mean inner CV metric of the winning hyperparameters after searching many settings on the same folds—that process is optimistically biased. For an unbiased estimate, use nested CV outer scores or a untouched test set; inner scores are for ranking during development only.

items.question: How do nested cross-validation inner and outer loops differ?
items.answer: The inner loop runs on each outer training pool to select hyperparameters or model type via CV. The outer loop holds out folds never used for that selection, refits the inner winner on the outer train pool, and scores once on the outer test fold. Report mean outer scores—not inner bests.

items.question: When choosing between two models with similar CV means, what else should you check?
items.answer: Compare fold-to-fold standard deviation. A model with slightly lower mean but much higher std is often overfit-prone under partition shifts. Prefer stable winners; confirm with repeated CV if means are within noise.
